_comment: DRAFT — Hip mobility and stability exercises. Not yet clinically reviewed. Do not integrate into runtime code.
_condition: Hip rehabilitation — mobility, post-surgical, hip replacement, tendinopathy

exercises.name: Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch
exercises.description: Kneel on one knee with the opposite foot forward. Shift your hips forward to feel a stretch in the front of the rear hip. Addresses hip flexor tightness — one of the most common contributors to anterior hip and lower back pain.
exercises.cue: Keep your trunk upright — do not lean forward. The stretch should be felt in the front of the hip, not the lower back.
exercises.painNote: Avoid deep hip flexor stretch immediately post hip surgery — check with your physio on safe range.
exercises.imageAlt: Person kneeling on one knee with opposite foot forward, hips shifted forward for a hip flexor stretch
contraindications: recent hip replacement (anterior approach — check protocol)
exercises.progressionNotes: Add a posterior pelvic tilt to increase the stretch on the rectus femoris.

exercises.name: Hip 90/90 Stretch
exercises.description: Sit on the floor with both hips at 90° — front leg in external rotation, back leg in internal rotation. Sit upright. Simultaneously stretches hip external rotators of one hip and internal rotators of the other.
exercises.cue: Sit as tall as possible — do not collapse through the lower back. Use a folded blanket under the hip if one side is significantly tighter.
exercises.painNote: Sharp pinching at the front of the hip in this position may indicate hip impingement — do not force the range. Consult your physio.
exercises.imageAlt: Person seated on floor with front leg at 90 degrees externally rotated and back leg internally rotated
exercises.progressionNotes: Add a forward lean over the front shin to intensify the external rotator stretch.

exercises.name: Hip Abduction — Sidelying
exercises.description: Lying on your side with the body in a straight line, lift the top leg upward maintaining a neutral foot position (toes pointing forward, not to ceiling). Isolates gluteus medius — the primary lateral hip stabiliser.
exercises.cue: Keep your toes pointing forward — not at the ceiling. If the toes roll up, you are using hip flexors instead of glute medius.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying on side lifting top leg with toes pointing forward
exercises.progressionNotes: Add a resistance band above the knees. Progress to standing hip abduction.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
exercises.description: Lying on your back with one knee bent and the other leg extended, drive the hips up using the bent leg only. Significantly greater demand on the glutes and core than a bilateral bridge. Also assesses hip symmetry.
exercises.cue: Keep your hips level — do not let the free hip drop. If it drops, regress to the bilateral bridge.
exercises.painNote: Lower back discomfort — check that you are driving through the glute, not extending the spine.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying on back with one leg extended, lifting hips with the bent knee
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to single-leg bridge with foot elevated on a step or bench.

exercises.name: Hip Circumduction
exercises.description: Standing holding a support, draw large circles with the knee — first clockwise, then counter-clockwise. Lubricates the hip joint, restores full passive hip range of motion, and warms up the capsule before loading exercises.
exercises.cue: Make the circles as big as is comfortable in all directions. You should feel a gentle mobilisation throughout the full circle.
exercises.painNote: Avoid forced hip flexion past 90° post hip replacement — adjust range accordingly.
exercises.imageAlt: Person standing on one leg drawing slow hip circles with the raised knee
exercises.progressionNotes: Increase circle size as ROM and comfort improve.

exercises.name: Hip Controlled Articular Rotation (CAR)
exercises.description: On all fours, perform a full 360° rotation of the hip through its maximum available range — bringing the knee to the chest, out to the side, behind, and back. A controlled articular rotation that trains end-range hip awareness and builds joint health.
exercises.tempo: Very slow and deliberate — 10 s per full rotation
exercises.cue: Move as slowly as possible. Pause and press into end range at every extreme of the arc — this is where the benefit is.
exercises.painNote: Joint clicking is common and not concerning unless accompanied by pain. Pinching at end range — reduce the arc.
exercises.imageAlt: Person on all fours moving one knee through a full circular arc
contraindications: acute hip replacement (modify range to within precautions)
exercises.progressionNotes: Perform standing (holding support) to increase demand on stabilisers.

exercises.name: Lateral Hip and ITB Stretch
exercises.description: Standing, cross one foot behind the other and lean sideways toward the front foot, reaching the arm overhead in the same direction. Lengthens the lateral hip, ITB, and TFL — often tight in runners and people with hip or knee pain.
exercises.cue: Keep your weight evenly on both feet. The stretch should be felt along the outer thigh and hip of the rear leg.
exercises.painNote: Avoid if this position causes hip or knee pain. The ITB is not truly stretchable — this stretch targets the TFL and lateral hip capsule.
exercises.imageAlt: Person standing with one foot crossed behind the other, leaning sideways with arm overhead
exercises.progressionNotes: Use a foam roller on the lateral thigh before this stretch to increase effectiveness.

exercises.name: Fire Hydrant
exercises.description: On hands and knees, lift one knee out to the side while maintaining the bent knee angle (90°). Targets the hip external rotators and gluteus medius simultaneously in a non-weight-bearing position.
exercises.cue: Do not rotate the pelvis — the hip is doing the work, not the lower back. Keep the core lightly engaged to stabilise.
exercises.imageAlt: Person on all fours lifting one knee out to the side like a dog at a fire hydrant
exercises.progressionNotes: Add resistance band above the knees.
